Challenge 2: Excluded Minors of GF⁢(pm)-representable matroids

Definition 1 (Matroid).

A matroid is a pair M=(E,ℐ) consisting of a finite set E and a collection ℐ of subsets of E, called the independent sets, such that:

  1. 1.

    ∅∈ℐ;

  2. 2.

    if I∈ℐ and J⊆I, then J∈ℐ;

  3. 3.

    if I,J∈ℐ and |I|<|J|, then there is some x∈J∖I such that I∪{x}∈ℐ.

The maximal independent sets of M are called the bases of M.

Definition 2 (GF⁢(pm)-representable matroid).

Let p be a prime number. Let m be a positive integer. Let GF⁢(pm) denote the finite field with pm elements. A matroid M is GF⁢(pm)-representable if there is a matrix A whose entries are in GF⁢(pm) and whose columns are labelled with the elements of M such that the independent sets of M are exactly the sets of columns of A which are linearly independent over GF⁢(pm).

Definition 3 (Matroid minor).

Let M be a matroid with ground set E. Given a subset D⊆E, the deletion of D from M, denoted M∖D, is the restriction of M to E∖D. The dual of M, denoted M∗, is the matroid with the same ground set as M, where a set is independent whenever its complement includes a base of M. Given a subset C⊆E, the contraction of M onto E∖C is the matroid M/C:=(M∗∖C)∗.

Given matroids M,N, then N is a minor of M if there exist disjoint sets C and D such that N=M/C∖D. A minor of M is proper if it is not equal to M.

Definition 4 (Excluded minor).

Let 𝒞 be a class of matroids closed under minors. A matroid M is an excluded minor for 𝒞 if M∉𝒞, but every proper minor of M belongs to 𝒞.

The class of GF⁢(pm)-representable matroids is closed under taking minors. Thus, it is natural to ask what its excluded minors are.

Open Problem 1.1.

Let p be a prime number. Let m be a positive integer. List the minimal excluded minors for the class of GF⁢(pm)-representable matroids and prove that the given list is complete up to isomorphism.

For pm=2, the excluded minor list is known: In 1958, Tutte proved that a matroid is representable over GF⁢(2) if and only if it has no minor isomorphic to U2,4 [6]. For pm=3, there is also a finite explicit list: In 1979, Bixby and Seymour proved that a matroid is representable over GF⁢(3) if and only if it has no minor isomorphic to U2,5, U3,5, F7, or F7∗ [1, 5]. The excluded minors for GF⁢(4)-representable matroids have been determined by Geelen, Gerards and Kapoor [2]. In general, Rota’s excluded-minor conjecture, proven by Geelen, Gerards and Whittle, says that for every finite field GF⁢(pm) there are only finitely many excluded minors for the class of GF⁢(pm)-representable matroids [4, 3].

We consider the following parametrized version of the well-known open problem to determine, for every finite field GF⁢(pm), the finitely many excluded minors explicitly.

Open Problem 1.2.

Let r be a positive integer. If there is a prime number p and a positive integer m such that r=pm, then list the excluded minors for the class of GF⁢(pm)-representable matroids and prove that the list is complete up to isomorphism.

References

  • [1] R. E. Bixby (1979) On Reid’s characterization of the ternary matroids. Journal of Combinatorial Theory, Series B 26 (2), pp. 174–204. doi: 10.1016/0095-8956(79)90056-X.
  • [2] J. F. Geelen, A. M. H. Gerards, and A. Kapoor (2000) The excluded minors for GF(4)-representable matroids. Journal of Combinatorial Theory, Series B 79 (2), pp. 247–299. doi: 10.1006/jctb.2000.1963.
  • [3] J. Geelen, B. Gerards, and G. Whittle (2014) Solving Rota’s conjecture. Notices of the American Mathematical Society 61 (7), pp. 736–743. doi: 10.1090/noti1139.
  • [4] G. Rota (1971) Combinatorial theory, old and new. In Actes du Congrès International des Mathématiciens, Nice, 1970, Vol. 3, Paris, pp. 229–233.
  • [5] P. D. Seymour (1979) Matroid representation over GF(3). Journal of Combinatorial Theory, Series B 26 (2), pp. 159–173. doi: 10.1016/0095-8956(79)90055-8.
  • [6] W. T. Tutte (1958) A homotopy theorem for matroids. I, II. Transactions of the American Mathematical Society 88, pp. 144–174. doi: 10.2307/1993243.
